findings.title: Compare accepts truncated or corrupt reports and fails open
findings.claim: The compare gate validates only the `tool` discriminator, not the report structure or numeric fields. A truncated report is accepted as an empty success, while Rust converts malformed percentages to zero. This defeats A1-16 and the compare gate’s primary purpose.
findings.reproduction: I gave both CLIs before/after files containing exactly `{"tool":"clankerprof_slices"}`. Both exited 0 and emitted `{"after_total_ns":0,"before_total_ns":0,"has_regression":false,"slices":[],"tool":"clankerprof_compare","top_improvements":[],"top_regressions":[]}`. I then gave Rust a before row `{"name":"A","pct":10}` and after row `{"name":"A","pct":"not-a-number"}`; it exited 0 and reported `after_pct: 0.0`, `delta_abs: -10.0`, `status: "improvement"`, and `has_regression: false`. These malformed inputs should exit 2 rather than produce a false-green comparison.

findings.title: NaN thresholds disable regression gating in both implementations
findings.claim: Neither CLI requires finite compare thresholds. IEEE NaN makes every threshold comparison false, turning an actual regression into a stable result with exit 0.
findings.reproduction: Using slice A at 10% before and 20% after, the normal command exited 2 with `has_regression: true`. Running the same inputs with Python `--threshold-abs nan` and Rust `--threshold-abs NaN` made both exit 0 and emit `{"before_pct":10.0,"after_pct":20.0,"delta_abs":10.0,"delta_rel":100.0,"status":"stable"}` with `has_regression: false`.

findings.title: Duplicate function names overwrite frame data in compare reports
findings.claim: Valid slice reports distinguish frames by function and filename, but compare converts them to a map keyed only by function and overwrites duplicates instead of aggregating them. Per-function deltas and top-mover lists can therefore hide regressions.
findings.reproduction: I compared slice A at 30% in both reports. Before frames were `f@/one=10`, `f@/two=15`, `g@/g=5`; after frames were `f@/one=15`, `f@/two=15`, `g@/g=0`. Both implementations exited 0 and reported function f as `before_pct:15.0`, `after_pct:15.0`, `delta_abs:0.0`, with `top_regressions:[]`. The actual per-function aggregate for f changed from 25 to 30 and should appear as a +5 regression.

findings.title: Facts-v2 numeric handling rejects valid IDs, coerces floats, and can panic
findings.claim: The facts readers do not enforce a consistent integer contract. Python silently truncates non-integral values, while Rust parses raw u64 IDs through i64 and stores aggregate time in i64, causing rejection or overflow on values Python handles.
findings.reproduction: Three probes reproduced this. (1) A valid v2 artifact using location/function ID `9223372036854775808` and sample value 7 made Python targets exit 0 with `total_time_ns: 7`; Rust exited 2 with `{"error":"Sample fact location_ids must be an array.","ok":false}` even though it was an array. (2) Changing `values` to `[7.9]` made Python exit 0 and report `cpu_time: 7`; Rust correctly rejected it. (3) Two samples each containing the valid signed-int64 value `9223372036854775807` made Python report `total_time_ns: 18446744073709551614`, while Rust exited 101 with `panicked ... attempt to add with overflow` at the iterator sum used by `facts.rs:290`.

findings.title: The promised JSON error boundary does not cover CLI usage or Python conversion errors
findings.claim: Argparse and clap terminate before the typed error handlers, and Python’s handler omits conversion exceptions such as OverflowError. Contracted failures consequently emit human usage text or tracebacks instead of one JSON envelope.
findings.reproduction: `.venv/bin/python -m clankerprof.cli targets --bogus` exited 2 with `usage: clankerprof ...` and `clankerprof: error: unrecognized arguments: --bogus`. `clankerprof-rs targets --bogus` likewise exited 2 with `error: unexpected argument '--bogus' found` and prose Usage output. Additionally, piping YAML `profile: /dev/null` plus `top: .inf` to Python `slices --config /dev/stdin` exited 1 with a traceback ending `OverflowError: cannot convert float infinity to integer`. The contract requires exit 2 and `{"ok":false,"error":...}` for all three cases.

findings.title: `--output` violates the documented receipt and global-option contract
findings.claim: Successful artifact writes must print a JSON receipt. Python instead prints the full artifact, Rust prints nothing, and Python compare cannot accept the documented global `--output` placement at all.
findings.reproduction: With a valid one-sample facts artifact, Python `targets --facts ... --target T --output /dev/null` exited 0 but stdout began with the complete `{"parents":...}` target report instead of `{"ok":true,"output":"/dev/null"}`. Rust ran the same command with exit 0 and zero stdout bytes. Python `clankerprof --output /dev/null compare --before ... --after ...` exited 2 with `unrecognized arguments: --output /dev/null`. Rust also rejects a global `clankerprof-rs --output /dev/null compare ...`.

findings.title: Published Rust CLI parity omits tracked slice-config and compare-alias surfaces
findings.claim: B3-05 is marked done and the parity document claims every listed capability, but Rust has no `slices --config` support or required `--focus-scopes` compare alias. The tracker note itself admits config merging was deferred.
findings.reproduction: Piping `profile: /dev/null` and `top: 1` to Python `slices --config /dev/stdin` exited 0 with a valid empty slice report. The identical Rust invocation exited 2 with `error: unexpected argument '--config' found`. Python accepted `compare ... --focus-scopes x` and exited 0; Rust exited 2 with `error: unexpected argument '--focus-scopes' found`. The Rust argument structs at the cited lines contain neither option.

findings.title: Rust silently ignores invalid scope predicates and `--by-slice` values
findings.claim: Rust contains two fail-open validation paths: scope predicate errors are discarded with `.ok().flatten()`, and unparsable `--by-slice` values cause no filtering. Python correctly rejects both inputs.
findings.reproduction: With a valid Leaf→T facts artifact and YAML `cost_kind: {Invalid: "bogus:value"}; scope: [{function: T}]`, Python scopes exited 2 with `{"error":"Unsupported predicate key: bogus","ok":false}`. Rust exited 0, categorized all 7 ns as `Other`, and emitted a complete report. Separately, Python slices with `--by-slice garbage` exited 2 with `invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'garbage'`; Rust exited 0 and returned the app slice with `total_time_ns: 7`.

findings.title: Rust path/glob matching is not compatible with Python
findings.claim: Rust recognizes `[` as a glob token but then escapes square brackets in its glob-to-regex conversion. Supported bracket-class globs silently produce different attribution from the Python reference.
findings.reproduction: Using a Leaf frame at `/app/a.rb`, parent T, and target config `{"T":{"Application":"path:/app/[ab].rb"}}`, Python exited 0 and emitted category `"name": "Application"`. Rust exited 0 on the same facts/config but emitted `"name": "Other"`. The discrepancy is deterministic and changes ownership attribution without warning.

findings.title: Rust silently drops later members from valid concatenated gzip profiles
findings.claim: Python decodes all members of a concatenated gzip stream, while Rust uses single-member `GzDecoder`. Rust therefore returns a successful but incomplete profile for valid gzip input.
findings.reproduction: I piped `gzip.compress(bytes.fromhex('3200')) + gzip.compress(bytes.fromhex('12021007'))` into each `facts --profile /dev/stdin --pretty` command. The decompressed protobuf contains an empty string-table entry followed by a sample with value 7. Python exited 0 with `sample_count: 1`, `empty_sample_count: 1`, and `total_primary_value: 7`. Rust also exited 0 but emitted `samples: []`, `sample_count: 0`, and `total_primary_value: 0`.

summary: Completion is refuted by 10 reproduced findings: one critical compare-gate bypass and nine major correctness, parity, and CLI-contract failures. `./bin/dev check` passed, including its Rust test lane. A separate `cargo test --workspace` invocation was sandbox-blocked opening `target/debug/.cargo-build-lock` with `Operation not permitted`; the same workspace tests ran successfully inside `./bin/dev check`, so that environmental failure is not reported as a repository defect.
